FUNDAMENTALS

Fundamental Analysis Skillset

💡 Investing is a hard practice, isn't it?

A lot of things must converge if we want to increase our chances of achieving a nice return.

And one of these things is certainly making a strong fundamental analysis to evaluate the potential dynamics that can impact the price: team, project, product, future prospects, tokenomics, etc.. In this lesson, I'm gonna show you how I personally make a fundamental analysis, the tools I use and the considerations that bring me to choose a project.

  • TAKE NOTE:

This practice is something that has to be made during the bear market when you’re choosing what you want to accumulate.

When the markup has started, everything depends on price action and nothing else.

1. The idea

The first thing I do is to check the idea behind the project, starting to create some questions in my mind:

  • Does it solve a real problem?

Success comes to those who can solve an issue. Solving a real problem has always brought success. And with real problem I'm not speaking about “real world problem”. We might arrive there in the future, once the sector will be fully regulated. I'm referring more to “crypto problems”, something that is circumscribed exclusively to crypto. Think about the cost of Ethereum gas fees. L2s went parabolic because they solved the problem.

  • Is it innovative?

If a project is recycling the same technology and idea of others, which are the probabilities that it can succeed? They are significantly reduced. Humans are attracted by new and unique solutions, they get bored fast of copies. "This L1 is incredibly scalable, it will allow making the cheapest and fastest transactions ever seen!" How many times did you hear that? You'll need 10 hands to count. Look for innovation.

  • Saturation of the market

Let's suppose that a project looks good but the field in which is working is full of competitors. The chances that it will emerge are minor. I look into superior technologies + a sector with fewer competitors. More probabilities of emerging and gaining traction.

2. Revenues

Crypto projects are first of all businesses and they need to generate revenues if they want to survive and succeed. They're made of humans which need to pay their bills, their food, and general expenses. They're not in a project for charity purposes. This brings us to make 2 important questions. How do they generate revenues? Is the company sustainable or just another Ponzi? Printing tokens to refund the company is NOT sustainable.

There are 2 ways you can check revenues :

  • Revenues section from TokenTerminal
  • Fees and revenues from DeFillama

Take note, projects revenues mostly come from:

  • Fees charged
  • Paid licenses
  • Selling products

Evaluating this metric is a key component.

3. The Team

Solid teams bring success, but lazy or shady ones bring to despair. A good team is crucial for a project to increase its chances of making it. Would you invest in something driven by a hardly credible man? What I do:

  • Go on the official website and check who is the team
  • Scan the team using Linkedin + Cypher Hunterto see past experiences
  • Check Twitter to see if they're active and update the community about developments or not.

Here’s CypherHunter’s screen searching for Polka Dot where we can see the key figures behind it. I always avoid teams that:

  • Don't have experience
  • Don't update the community frequently
  • Refuse criticism

Always make sure to navigate between Telegram, Discord and Twitter to see how they respond. Arrogant replies = Sayonara

4. The Community

A strong community is a crucial factor to understand how much people believe in a project. It also helps to form new potential holders. People interested in a project will ask about how it works, which the future plans are and so on. Strong and friendly community : new holder achieved. Check how much the community engages by scrolling through Telegram and Twitter pages of that project.

Bonus tip:

  • Open Lunar Crush
  • Click on the research bar and put the name of the project.
  • Scroll down until you find social mentions and social engagements.
  • If the rate is high you'll know that this will contribute to gauging the interest.

It's another metric to add to the community indicator. If we take Kujira as an example we can see on the tab left an increase in social mentions, engagements, dominance, contributors and so on. Great sign for a project.

5. Token & Tokenomics

This is a crucial factor. The majority make bad similarities between the stock market and the crypto market. Remember: when you buy a token you're not buying a share and without proper tokenomics success is not guaranteed even if the project is outstanding. Many gaming projects have seen a huge rise in the past but over time they fell hard. People were playing games, earning tokens and then cashing out. No incentives and a rising token supply have suppressed the price over time. That's why tokenomics is important, no matter the project.

Tools I use for tokenomics evaluation:

  • Checking supply: Coingecko + CoinMarketCap
  • Vesting schedule: TokenUnlocks + VestLab
  • Utility: Whitepaper
  • Token allocation: Whitepaper + etherscan| holders section | token holders chart.

An unfair distribution of a token it's usually a red flag. Big entities and whales that have large quantities of tokens can dump causing a major liquidation cascade.

6. TVL

TVL means total value locked which is a good measure to gauge the health of a DeFi ecosystem. It includes all the coins deposited in all the functions that DeFi protocols offer, including staking, lending, and liquidity pools.

This is a key metric. No TVL & no interest in the ecosystem & hard that the price will rise. How to check it? Simple, use DefiLlama and scroll over projects TVL to gauge the money that is flowing into them.

7. Liquidity

Liquidity is a metric mainly composed of 3 things:

  • Exchanges where to buy a coin.

Simple equation: fewer exchanges → less liquidity & less access for people to buy the coin → less potential upward moves.

  • Enough liquidity to guarantee buy and sell operations

Let’s take SMG for example. Independently of the strenght and the vision of the project we can notice a big red flag: volumes are insanely low (242$/24H). There’s no liquidity to buy/sell at the moment, and this can compromise our operativity.

  • Potential major exchange listings: when a coin is listed on less-known exchanges, the listing on a major one can boost the price. (early buyers look for selling their coins at listings/late buyers chase the pump)

"Sir, I don't know where to find liquidity metric" CoinGecko has everything.

  • Trading volume
  • Exchanges listings

8. Product

Projects need to add value to this world if they want to succeed and to do this they need to provide products or services that people like or want to use.

Case study: Solana

Solana went parabolic (48000%) during the last bullrun due to many catalysts:

  • Market conditions (altseason)
  • Overall frenzy
  • NFT hype
  • Usecase

As the NFT world took off, the Solana chain was perfectly optimized for fast and cheap transactions. The most important NFTs projects were built on Solana and people only relied on that chain to move their NFTs. This has increased the use of the product contributing, together with the above factors to skyrocketing the price.

I always try to test (where it's possible) the products of a crypto project. Dex, Cex, platforms, games, blockchains.. If I don't like something as a product, most of the time I never invest in it. Also, I don't rely on others' opinions. Don't trust, verify if is truly good or not.

9. Risk/Reward ratio

How much ROl do you need to be satisfied from your investment? Set mental targets and be realistic. Mid/low caps are risky plays but have much more upside potential than a higher cap. If you pick a high cap you can't expect ×50/×100. It's nearly impossible.

10. Price

I always look at the price action of a coin before buying, no matter how revolutionary a project can look.

  • Buy during accumulation
  • Sell during the mark up/distribution phases
  • Do nothing during the mark-down

If you’re late to a coin just WAIT for another opportunity, don’t rush if you don’t have in mind a clear strategy. Plan → execute

11. Key takeaways

  • Altcoins have higher risks

I always set up a budget depending on the risk of the coin. For small-caps I set up a 5% maximum, for mid-cap it can go to 15/20% depending on the season. Make sure to NEVER go all-in. Risk management is key.

  • Pick up your poison

You'll never find the perfect project. Each project has points of strength and points of weakness. Centralization, utilities, lack of marketing. Your goal should be to evaluate them and pick the poison that hurts the less.

  • DeFi protocols need to be safe

How many times we've heard about exploits in DeFi? If you're evaluating a DeFi protocol you should focus on its security. You can help your research by using DefiSafety which rates protocols depending on their security.

  • No marketing, no party

You can have the best technology and the best team but if no one knows about you, it's hard to emerge. Make sure to check if a project launches marketing campaigns. Take note that most of the projects launch campaings during bull markets for hype.

  • Timelines

What happens if you don't go to work for 1 month? Your boss fires you. Always take note of the progress of a team, if they respect the timelines and the roadmap. A delay can happen. Multiple delays and excuses can be identified as a red flag.

  • Stay updated

Once you decided that a project is good and you bought your first tokens, don't forget about it. Stay constantly updated on developments, achievements and announcements. Flowers don't grow up if you don't water them regularly.

  • Final recap

To conclude, fundamental analysis is key to evaluating the potential strengths or weaknesses of a project. Make sure to match everything:

  • Team
  • Revenues
  • Products
  • Community
  • Future prospects
  • Marketing
  • Tokenomics
  • ROI
  • Liquidity
  • Fresh chart
